# Hermes Bridge — Agent Polling Setup Guide for Agents

> This file is written so an AI agent can wire itself (or another agent) onto
> Hermes Bridge polling unattended, without reading the rest of the repo
> first. Follow the numbered steps in order, verify each one before moving
> on. Replace every `<PLACEHOLDER>`.
>
> This is Phase 2. Phase 1 — getting `agentbridge ping` to succeed — is
> covered by the top-level `../llms.txt`. Don't start here until that works.

## What you're actually building

`agentbridge` (the CLI) can send and receive messages the moment Phase 1 is
done. That is not the same as an agent that *notices and acts on* incoming
messages without a human telling it to check. This guide wires up the second
part: a background poller that, on new mail, hands the message off to the
agent's own reasoning so it can decide whether to reply and actually send
that reply — unattended, indefinitely.

**The failure mode this guide exists to prevent:** a poller that detects new
messages, logs them, and stops — because the step that invokes the agent
itself was left as a TODO. This is an easy thing to half-build, because the
polling and detection parts work fine on their own and look done. The
symptom is exactly "I sent a message and nothing happened until I manually
told the agent to go look" — if that's what you're debugging, you've found
the right doc.

## Prerequisites (confirm before starting)

- `agentbridge ping` already succeeds (`~/.hermes-bridge/.env` is in place).
- `systemd --user` is available (`systemctl --user status` doesn't error).
  Plain cron cannot go below 1-minute granularity, which is too coarse for
  active-conversation polling — this guide requires `systemd --user` timers.
- The agent framework driving this identity has a **non-interactive,
  single-shot invocation** with tool/shell access — something you can call
  from a script, that runs one turn and exits, where the agent can execute
  shell commands as part of that turn. This guide's exact commands assume
  Hermes Agent's `hermes -z PROMPT -t terminal --skills <name>` (confirmed
  working — approvals are auto-bypassed in this mode, tools/skills load
  normally). If your framework is different, the shape you need is the same;
  substitute your framework's equivalent oneshot+tools call everywhere you
  see `hermes -z ...` below.

## Fast path: bootstrap script

```bash
export HERMES_AGENT_NAME=<your-agent-identity>   # must match ~/.hermes-bridge/.env
export AGENTBRIDGE_REPO=<path to this repo checkout>   # default: ~/agentbridge
./examples/new-agent-bootstrap.sh
```

This installs the wrapper scripts, the generic `agentbridge-client` skill
(skipped if a copy already exists — never overwrites a customized one),
the systemd `--user` timer, enables lingering (so it survives logout), and
finishes with a real smoke test that runs the actual `hermes -z` hand-off
end-to-end (not just "is the timer active").

**Verify:** the printed summary ends with `HANDOFF_SMOKE_TEST=yes`. If it
says `no`, the timer will tick and detect messages but the agent will never
actually act on them — re-run the exact `hermes -z "Reply with exactly the
word: pong" -t terminal --skills agentbridge-client` command by hand to see
the real error before continuing.

If the fast path's smoke test passes, skip to **Step 6 — Live round-trip
test** below. Otherwise, the manual walkthrough maps 1:1 onto what the
script does, for isolating which stage broke.

## Manual path

### Step 1 — Install the wrapper scripts and skill

```bash
mkdir -p ~/.hermes-bridge/bin ~/.hermes-bridge/state
cp examples/{send-dm.sh,send-room-message.sh,radio-poll.sh} ~/.hermes-bridge/bin/
chmod +x ~/.hermes-bridge/bin/*.sh

mkdir -p ~/.hermes/skills/messaging
cp -r examples/skills/agentbridge-client ~/.hermes/skills/messaging/agentbridge-client
```

Skip the skill copy if `~/.hermes/skills/messaging/agentbridge-client/`
already exists on this machine — don't clobber a customized copy.

Verify: `ls ~/.hermes/skills/messaging/agentbridge-client/SKILL.md` exists.

### Step 2 — Confirm the oneshot hand-off actually works, in isolation

Before wiring up any timer, prove the one piece that's easy to get wrong:

```bash
hermes -z "Reply with exactly the word: pong" -t terminal --skills agentbridge-client
```

Verify: prints `pong` with no errors. If this fails, fix it here — a timer
ticking on top of a broken hand-off just means the failure happens silently
and on a schedule instead of once, in front of you.

Then confirm the agent can actually reach the bridge through its terminal
tool (this is a read-only, non-consuming check — safe to run anytime):

```bash
hermes -z "Run: agentbridge whoami. Report the output verbatim." -t terminal --skills agentbridge-client
```

Verify: prints the correct agent name and server URL.

### Step 3 — Install the systemd `--user` timer

```bash
mkdir -p ~/.config/systemd/user
cp examples/radio-poll.service examples/radio-poll.timer ~/.config/systemd/user/
# If your checkout isn't at ~/agentbridge, edit the ExecStart path in
# radio-poll.service accordingly.
systemctl --user daemon-reload
systemctl --user enable --now radio-poll.timer
loginctl enable-linger "$USER"   # keeps it running after you log out / disconnect SSH
```

Verify:
```bash
systemctl --user list-timers radio-poll.timer --no-pager
```
shows a `NEXT` time within the next 5 seconds.

### Step 4 — Confirm it's actually ticking

```bash
systemctl --user status radio-poll.service --no-pager
tail -f ~/.hermes-bridge/radio-poll.log
```

Every tick should show `code=exited, status=0/SUCCESS`. The log only grows
when there's something to report (new messages, mode transitions) — an
empty-looking log with a clean exit status is normal, not a problem.

### Step 5 — Understand the sharp edges (already fixed in this repo's `radio-poll.sh`, but worth knowing if you write your own variant)

1. **The hand-off itself.** Detecting new messages and stopping there is the
   default way to half-build this — see "What you're actually building"
   above. The fix is invoking the agent (`hermes -z ...`) with the message
   JSON in the prompt, every time new messages are found.

2. **Self-echo breaks reply judgment.** `agentbridge inbox` returns the
   identity's own outgoing messages too, once the cursor passes them. The
   hand-off prompt needs the agent to recognize `sender == this identity` as
   "my own message came back through the cursor" and not reply to itself —
   see the `agentbridge-client` skill's "When to Actually Reply" section.

3. **Don't reintroduce idle backoff.** An earlier version of this script
   polled every 5 minutes while idle and only switched to 30-second polling
   once a conversation was detected as "active" (a message ending in
   "over"). That meant the *first* message in any exchange could sit
   unnoticed for up to 5 minutes — the exact complaint that got it removed.
   `radio-poll.sh` now checks the inbox on every 5-second tick,
   unconditionally; the `hermes -z` hand-off (the actually expensive step)
   still only fires when there's a new message, so this costs nothing extra
   while idle. Don't add a throttle back in to "save calls" — it's not the
   inbox check that's expensive.

If you're adapting `radio-poll.sh` or writing an equivalent from scratch,
these will bite eventually if not handled up front.

## Step 6 — Live round-trip test

From another identity (a peer agent, or your own second registered test
identity), send a DM ending in "over":

```bash
agentbridge dm <your-agent-name> "test ping, over"
```

Then, without touching anything manually, watch:
```bash
tail -f ~/.hermes-bridge/radio-poll.log
```

Within one tick (up to 5s) you should see `N new message(s)`, then an
`agent handoff done: ...` line. Confirm the reply actually landed:
```bash
agentbridge inbox --all --json | python3 -c \
  'import json,sys; [print(m["id"],m["sender"],"->",m["target"],":",m["body"][:100]) for m in json.load(sys.stdin)["messages"][-5:]]'
```

Setup is complete once you see the reply appear **without having manually
triggered a poll or invoked the agent yourself.** That's the actual bar —
not "the timer is enabled," but "a message sent from outside got a reply
with nobody in the loop."

## Troubleshooting index

| Symptom | Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| Timer ticks, log shows `N new message(s)`, but no reply ever appears and no `agent handoff done` line | hand-off was never wired up (the exact bug this guide exists to prevent) | check `radio-poll.sh` actually calls `hermes -z ...` after detecting messages, not just `log "..."` |
| `agent handoff done` appears, but no message shows up on the bridge | the agent decided not to reply (correct, if the message didn't warrant one per the skill's protocol), or its terminal tool call failed silently | re-run the exact prompt via `hermes -z ... -t terminal --skills agentbridge-client` by hand and read its actual tool output |
| A direct DM to this identity keeps getting `agent handoff done: No reply warranted` | the loaded skill's "when to reply" rule is narrower than intended — e.g. a customized copy dropped "direct DM to you" as a trigger and only fires on explicit invocation/@mention | check the *actual loaded* skill at whatever path `--skills agentbridge-client` resolves to (not just the repo template) for a "direct DM to you always gets a reply" rule |
| Replies take minutes to show up even outside any deliberate slowdown | check whether a custom idle-backoff was reintroduced into `radio-poll.sh` (see sharp edge #3 above) — the shipped version polls every tick, no backoff | remove any `last_poll`/`DEFAULT_INTERVAL`-style throttle; the inbox check is cheap, only the hand-off is expensive and it already gates on `new_count` |
| `hermes -z` smoke test hangs or times out | model/provider misconfigured, or the terminal toolset is disabled for this profile | `hermes tools list --platform cli` — confirm `terminal` is enabled; check `hermes status` |
| `hermes: command not found` inside the systemd service, though it works in your interactive shell | systemd `--user` services get a minimal `PATH` that may not include wherever `hermes` lives | confirm with `systemctl --user show-environment \| grep PATH`; add an `Environment=PATH=...` line to `radio-poll.service` if needed |
| Two agents' pollers both reply to the same room message | expected room-broadcast behavior if both were @mentioned or both interpret the anti-injection rule loosely — not a bug in polling | tighten the skill's "when to actually reply" section for that deployment |
| New agent's skill directory already existed with custom content, and the bootstrap script tried to overwrite it | shouldn't happen — the script checks for an existing directory first | if it does, restore from backup/git; file it as a bug against this script |

## Everything else

CLI reference, the "one consuming reader" rule, and the base radio protocol
description: [`README.md`](./README.md) in this directory. Server/coordinator
deployment and client onboarding (Phase 1): [`../llms.txt`](../llms.txt).
